title: Restoring Velocity Immunity via Dynamic Mirror Compensation in a Large-Area Dual-Atom-Interferometer Gyroscope
authors: Jie Gu, Yin-fei Mao, Zhan-Wei Yao, An-qing Zhang, Si-Bin Lu, Shao-kang Li, Min Jiang, Xiao-Li Chen, Min Ke, Xi Chen, Run-Bing Li, Jin Wang, Ming-Sheng Zhan
abstract: We propose and demonstrate a dynamical mirror compensation scheme to restore velocity immunity in a large-area dual-atom-interferometer gyroscope. In an ideal Mach-Zehnder configuration, the phase shift is inherently immune to atomic velocity, but this property is broken by the Earth's rotation via the Coriolis effect. We overcome this by actively rotating the Raman mirrors during the pulse sequence to cancel the time-dependent angular offset. The implementation relies on a decouplable calibration-compensation chain to remove rotation-induced time-dependent terms. The scheme is validated on a dual-atom-interferometer gyroscope with an interference area of 21.1 cm^2. After compensation, the phase's dependence on atomic velocity is reduced 40-fold, and the velocity contribution to scale-factor stability is evaluated to be 0.13 ppm. The sensor achieves a rotation sensitivity of 1.3\times10^{-8} rad/s/Hz^{1/2} and a stability of 1.9\times10^{-10} rad/s at 4500 s integration, together with a common-mode noise rejection ratio of up to 459, demonstrated in a seismic event. This work removes a key obstacle to scale-factor stabilization in atom-interferometer gyroscopes and paves the way for their applications in inertial navigation and geophysics.
summary: The research introduces a dynamic mirror compensation technique to correct for velocity-dependent phase shifts in large-area atom interferometers caused by Earth's rotation. By actively compensating for the Coriolis effect, the authors restored velocity immunity, significantly improving the gyroscope's stability and paving the way for its use in high-precision navigation and geophysics.
main_problem: The gyroscope's inherent velocity immunity, crucial for accurate inertial sensing, is broken by the Coriolis effect induced by Earth's rotation in large-area atom interferometers.
main_result: The authors successfully implemented a dynamic mirror compensation scheme, reducing the phase's velocity dependence by a factor of 40 and achieving a high rotation sensitivity of $1.3 	imes 10^{-8} 	ext{ rad/s}/	ext{Hz}^{1/2}$.
method: They actively rotated the Raman mirrors during the pulse sequence to cancel the time-dependent angular offset caused by the Coriolis effect, using a multi-stage calibration process.
model_or_system: The experiment uses a large-area dual-atom-interferometer gyroscope based on $^{87}	ext{Rb}$ atoms in a Mach-Zehnder configuration. The system's phase shift is analyzed for its dependence on rotation ($\Omega$) and atomic velocity ($v'$).
key_observables: Interference phase ($\delta\varphi_a, \delta\varphi_b$), fringe contrast ($C$), rotation phase ($\varphi_\Omega$), and the measured stability/sensitivity ($	ext{rad/s}/	ext{Hz}^{1/2}$).
important_parameters: Interference area of $21.1 	ext{ cm}^2$; velocity dependence reduction factor of 40; stability of $1.9 	imes 10^{-10} 	ext{ rad/s}$ at $4500 	ext{ s}$.
main_assumptions: The method relies on a 'decouplable calibration-compensation chain' involving contrast, phase, and dynamic compensation steps to isolate and null systematic errors.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the apparatus schematic, the restoration of maximum fringe contrast after compensation, and the comparison of the rotation phase slope vs. interrogation time before and after compensation.
paper_structure: The paper details the physical setup, identifies the Coriolis-induced systematic error, proposes the dynamic mirror compensation scheme across three calibration stages, presents quantitative results showing the restoration of velocity immunity, and concludes with performance metrics and future outlook.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses a major systematic error source (Coriolis coupling) in advanced quantum sensing platforms, providing a robust experimental technique for achieving higher precision in inertial measurements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Principle of experimental. (a) Schematic of the dual large-area atom-interferometer gyroscope. (b) Dynamic mirror compensation scheme: black mirrors-Earth-fixed frame; green/purple-mirror angles seen by the 1st loop and 2nd one before compensation, and red-mirror angles after compensation in inertial frame. (c) Maximum contrast of the first (black squares) and third (red dots) mirror angle for a given setting of the other mirror, and the inset is the numerical simulation result.
FIG. 3: (color online) Rotation phase versus interrogation time T before (black squares) and after (red dots) dynamic compensation.
FIG. 2: (color online) Fringe contrast versus mirror angle (a) before and (b) after dynamic compensation for the 1st loop (black squares) and 2nd one (red dots).

FIG. 4: (color online) Allan deviations of the dual atom in- terferometers. Black squares: 1st interferometer; red trian- gles: 2nd interferometer; blue dots: rotation signal; black line: τ −1/2 scaling of 13 nrad/s/Hz1/2; orange line: detection noise limit corresponding to 7.3 nrad/s/Hz1/2; violet curve: phase drift caused by AC-Stark shifts; dark yellow curve: phase drift induced by wavefront distortions.

FIG. 5: (color online) Seismic signal recorded by the dual atom interferometers. Black: seismometer weighted by the transfer function; red, blue: individual interferometer phases; green: rotation phase; orange: smoothed rotation phase.
